title: Borscht
subtitle: Ukrainian beetroot and vegetable soup
excerpt: A thick, vivid red soup of beetroot, cabbage, potato, and carrot in a tangy tomato broth. The soup that defines Eastern European cooking — earthy, substantial, and deeply coloured. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Borscht is the signature soup of Ukraine, Poland, and the wider Eastern European Jewish tradition. The version here is the classic hot Ukrainian borshch with beetroot, cabbage, and potato. Documented by Olia Hercules in Mamushka (2015) and by Claudia Roden in The Book of Jewish Food (1997). Cold borscht (beet gazpacho) is a separate dish.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze for up to three months. The colour fades slightly on reheating — add a teaspoon of red wine vinegar when serving to brighten the flavour.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles easily. Improves on the second day.

content.text: The colour of borscht comes from the beetroot, and the beetroot loses its colour quickly in hot liquid. The trick is to add the beetroot near the end of cooking and finish with vinegar, which fixes the colour and sharpens the flavour at the same time. Beetroot cooked from the start turns the soup brown rather than red.

items.prepNote: peeled and diced into 1 cm pieces

content.text: Heat {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a large saucepan over medium heat. Cook {{onion}} finely chopped onion and {{carrot}} grated carrot for 8 minutes until soft. Add {{garlic}} of garlic and stir for 1 minute. Add the shredded cabbage and cook for 5 minutes. Add {{tinned-tomatoes}} of hand-crushed tomatoes and {{stock-vegetable}} of vegetable stock. Add the potato and {{bay-leaves}} bay leaves. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, and simmer for 20 minutes until the potato is tender. Stir in the grated beetroot and simmer for 10 minutes. Add {{red-wine-vinegar}} of red wine vinegar. Taste and adjust seasoning. Remove the bay leaves. Serve with a generous spoonful of sour cream and fresh dill.

items.cause: Beetroot added too early and cooked too long in the hot stock.
items.fix: Add the raw grated beetroot in the last 10 minutes of cooking only. The colour holds if the beetroot does not overcook.

items.symptom: Flavour flat and earthy without brightness
items.cause: Not enough vinegar, or vinegar added too early.
items.fix: Add the vinegar at the very end, taste, and add more as needed. The soup should have a noticeable acidic edge.
Wear disposable gloves when peeling and grating raw beetroot. The staining is persistent. Cutting boards can be cleaned immediately with lemon juice.

content.text: Borscht is the soup of Ukraine, Poland, Russia, and the Ashkenazi Jewish diaspora. Each tradition has its own version: Ukrainian borshch is thick and hearty, often with meat; Polish barszcz is clear and served with small dumplings; the Jewish version is often cold and served with sour cream in summer. The word entered the English language through the Jewish diaspora in America, where it became associated with the Catskill Mountain resort circuit in New York, where it was a staple of the hotels that Jewish families escaped to in summer — so much so that the area was nicknamed the Borscht Belt.
